_notes: Auto-generated by scripts/classify_districts.py (Stage 4) produced an EMPTY rule set: the pack's scraped breadcrumbs are only 2 levels deep (chapter 'Chapter 35.2 - ZONING ORDINANCE' -> article, e.g. 'ARTICLE IV. - BASE ZONING DISTRICTS'), with no division-level breakdown, so Stage 4's article-level grouping lumped all 14 base zoning districts (R-C, R-1, R-2, R-3, R-4, B-1, B-3, B-4, B-5, IN-1, IN-2, I-1, I-2, I-3 -- 59 sections total) into one heterogeneous 'BASE ZONING DISTRICTS' bucket and correctly refused to assign it a single district ('governs base zoning districts with multiple district types mentioned, such as R-C and R-1, making it cross-cutting and broad in scope', confidence 0.6). Human-reviewed 2026-07-18: unlike fredericksburg-va (one section per district), Lynchburg subdivides EACH base district into 4-6 separate sections (Sec. 35.2-XX.1 Purpose, .2 Authorized uses, .3 Use standards, .4 Development standards, plus .5/.6 for the two institutional districts) that share a district-scoped section-number prefix but whose titles ('Sec. 35.2-42.2. - Authorized uses.') do NOT repeat the district code, so a single per-district title_contains rule (e.g. 'r-2 district') would only catch the purpose/use-standards/development-standards sections and silently miss the 'Authorized uses' section for every district. Added 14 title_contains rules keyed on each district's section-number prefix instead (e.g. title_contains: '35.2-42.' catches Sec. 35.2-42.1 through .4, all four R-2 sections), following the numeric-prefix precedent already used by loudoun-county-va's Use Table rules ('3.02.01' etc.) rather than the district-name-in-title precedent used by fredericksburg-va, because Lynchburg's own title text doesn't support the name-based approach. Confirmed against ordinance body text and the city's live ArcGIS zoning services (mapviewer.lynchburgva.gov, Planning/ZoningAndLandUse and Apps/ParcelViewer/MapServer/42, unified 'Zone' attribute values R1/R2/R3/R4/RC/B1/B3/B4/B5/IN1/IN2/I1/I2/I3 with no punctuation, matching the ordinance's hyphenated codes 1:1). Residential districts R-C, R-1, R-2, R-3, R-4 (Sec. 35.2-40.x through 35.2-44.x) all collapse to 'residential-low-density' -- the closed DISTRICT_VOCAB has no separate low/medium/high-density residential tiers, same precedent as every other pack (fredericksburg collapsed 7 residential tiers the same way). R-C's purpose text ('very low intensity development... stream valleys... steep slope... open spaces') reads more like a conservation overlay than farmland, and DISTRICT_VOCAB does have a bare 'agricultural' term (used by henrico-county-va for its conservation/agricultural division), but R-C's authorized uses are still single-household residential development as the base use (it is officially an 'R' residential code, not an 'A' agricultural code, and has no farm/agricultural-operation purpose language), so it was kept as residential-low-density rather than reclassified to agricultural -- flagged as a judgment call. Commercial/office districts B-1 (neighborhood-scale office/retail/service, pedestrian-oriented) and B-3 (community-scale office/retail/service) and B-5 (large-site commercial with nuisance-buffering siting) -> 'commercial-employment'. B-4 (Sec. 35.2-47.x) is a JUDGMENT CALL: mapped to 'mixed-use-core' rather than 'commercial-employment' because its purpose text explicitly describes 'a wide range of residential, commercial and other sales and service operations... in downtown Lynchburg and other mixed use centers throughout the City' and 'a compatible mix of multi-household residences, business and related activities... at relatively high densities', and its use standards prohibit drive-in/drive-through businesses and hiring halls -- classic downtown pedestrian-oriented mixed-use signatures, not general commercial. This was GIS-verified: the live ArcGIS Business sublayer and the unified Zoning layer both show a B4 parcel at 400 Clay St in downtown Lynchburg (round-trip point-in-polygon confirmed against two independent services). IN-1 and IN-2 (Sec. 35.2-49.x, 35.2-50.x) are a second JUDGMENT CALL: both are 'institutional' campus districts (schools, colleges, hospitals, senior living, churches, 'multiple buildings contained in a campus setting') and were mapped to 'commercial-employment' on the same reasoning fredericksburg-va used for its Planned Development-Medical Center district -- a hospital/university campus is an employment-and-services center, not a zero-employee use, and DISTRICT_VOCAB has no separate institutional tier -- but unlike B-4 there is no IN-1/IN-2 fixture in parcel_fixtures.json verifying this on the ground, so this mapping should be treated with more caution than the others. Industrial districts I-1 (moderate/low-density, high design standards), I-2 (low-nuisance), I-3 (highest-nuisance-tolerance) -> 'industrial-zone'. Left unmapped (unknown/general), matching the 'don't force overlay/topical articles into the vocab' precedent from henrico-county-va and fredericksburg-va: ARTICLE V - SPECIAL ZONING DISTRICTS in its entirety, including the FSC (Fifth Street Corridor) and SC (a second named corridor) special districts -- both are geographically-scoped corridor/master-plan overlay districts ('protect and promote the vision and goals of the Fifth Street Corridor Master Plan', 'enhance the visual appearance of the corridor'), not general-purpose base districts -- plus Airport Safety Zones, Historic Overlay Districts, and the Floodplain Area Regulations subdivision, all of which layer on top of a base district rather than being one; ARTICLE II (Procedures), ARTICLE III (Zoning Districts, Maps and Boundaries -- general provisions ABOUT the districts, not a district itself), ARTICLE VI (Site Development Standards), ARTICLE VII (Specific Land Use Standards), ARTICLE VIII (Land Use Development Patterns), ARTICLE IX (Non-Conforming Situations), ARTICLE X (Administration), and ARTICLE XI (Definitions) -- these are genuinely cross-district standards/procedural articles, not districts, matching how other wave-2/3 packs left their equivalent articles unmapped. Rules are first-match.
